title: SEGMENT 9 — The biomarker problem: PD-L1, TMB, and MSI

lines.text: SEGMENT 9 — The biomarker problem: PD-L1, TMB, and MSI
We have arrived at the question that the field cannot stop arguing about.
Who responds to checkpoint inhibitors?
Because here is the uncomfortable fact.
For all the magic of anti-PD-1 therapy, only twenty to forty percent of patients with most cancers actually respond.
The other sixty to eighty percent get nothing, or worse — they get toxicity without benefit.

Given that these drugs cost over a hundred and fifty thousand dollars per patient per year, and given that patients with advanced cancer have limited time, the question of who to treat is not academic.
It is life-and-death and it is billions of dollars and it is fundamentally unsolved.
What we have is a handful of imperfect biomarkers.
Let me walk through the three main ones.
Biomarker one: PD-L1 expression on the tumor, measured by immunohistochemistry.
This is the most widely used.
The pathologist takes a biopsy of the tumor, stains it with an antibody against PD-L1, and counts what fraction of tumor cells — or sometimes tumor cells plus immune cells — stain positive.
The result is reported as a percentage, often using a metric called the Tumor Proportion Score (TPS) or the Combined Positive Score (CPS).
The logic is intuitive.
If a tumor expresses lots of PD-L1, it's actively using the PD-1/PD-L1 brake.
Blocking that brake should help.
If a tumor doesn't express PD-L1, the brake isn't being pressed, and the drug has nothing to do.
In practice, PD-L1 is a flawed biomarker.
It's useful — patients with high PD-L1 expression are more likely to respond on average — but the correlation is fuzzy.
Some patients with PD-L1-negative tumors respond.
Some patients with PD-L1-very-high tumors don't respond.
The assays differ between manufacturers — there are at least four different antibody clones used clinically, each with different cutoffs, each with different scoring rules.
PD-L1 expression is heterogeneous within a tumor, so a single biopsy might miss positive regions.
And PD-L1 expression changes over time, especially under treatment.
It's a snapshot biomarker for a moving target.
Still, in some indications — notably first-line lung cancer — PD-L1 expression is the gatekeeper.
Pembrolizumab monotherapy is approved for advanced non-small-cell lung cancer only in patients with TPS greater than or equal to fifty percent.
Below that threshold, you need to add chemotherapy.
The biomarker drives the regimen.
Biomarker two: tumor mutational burden, or TMB.
TMB is, basically, a count of how many mutations the tumor has.
You sequence the tumor's exome and count the non-synonymous mutations per megabase of DNA.
Cancers with more mutations have more neoantigens — more weird peptides for T cells to recognize — and so, in principle, more material for the immune system to attack.
TMB does correlate with response to checkpoint inhibitors.

Melanoma and lung cancer, the two great success stories of immunotherapy, are among the most highly mutated cancers — because melanoma is driven by UV-induced DNA damage and lung cancer by tobacco-induced damage, both of which generate enormous mutational loads.
Pancreatic cancer and prostate cancer, by contrast, have low TMB, and they respond poorly to immunotherapy.
In 2020, the FDA approved pembrolizumab for any solid tumor with high TMB — defined as ten or more mutations per megabase — based on the KEYNOTE-158 trial.
It was the second tumor-agnostic approval (we'll get to the first in a moment).
But TMB has been controversial as a biomarker.
The cutoff is somewhat arbitrary.
Different sequencing panels give different TMB numbers.
And not every high-TMB tumor responds.
It's a better-than-nothing biomarker, not a perfect one.
Biomarker three: microsatellite instability, MSI, or its molecular cousin mismatch-repair deficiency, dMMR.
This is the one biomarker that approaches predictive perfection, in the cancers where it applies.
Microsatellites are short, repetitive DNA sequences scattered across the genome — things like "CACACACA...
" or "GTGTGTGT...
" When DNA replicates, these repetitive sequences are easy to misreplicate; the polymerase slips and adds or drops a repeat unit.
Normally, a system called mismatch repair catches these slips and fixes them.
If mismatch repair is broken — if the genes MLH1, MSH2, MSH6, or PMS2 are mutated or epigenetically silenced — then the slips accumulate, and you get microsatellite instability.
The microsatellites in the tumor's DNA have different lengths than in the patient's normal DNA.
You can detect this by PCR or by sequencing.

Tumors with MSI-high status — about fifteen percent of colorectal cancers, a smaller fraction of many other cancers — have absolutely enormous mutational burdens, often hundreds of mutations per megabase.
They generate vast neoantigen repertoires.
They are highly immunogenic.
And — this is the punchline — they respond to PD-1 blockade at extraordinary rates.
Forty to sixty percent objective response rates, with durable responses, in cancers that would otherwise be chemorefractory and lethal.

In 2017, based primarily on the work of Dung Le and Luis Diaz at Johns Hopkins, the FDA approved pembrolizumab for any unresectable or metastatic MSI-high or dMMR solid tumor that had progressed on prior therapy.
Any such tumor.
Colon, endometrial, gastric, small bowel, pancreatic, prostate, bladder, anything.
It was the first time the FDA had ever approved a cancer drug based purely on a molecular biomarker, regardless of the organ of origin.
The first tumor-agnostic approval in oncology.
This was a tectonic shift.
For its entire history, oncology had organized itself by the organ of origin.
There were breast cancer doctors and lung cancer doctors and colon cancer doctors.
Drugs were approved for breast cancer or lung cancer or colon cancer.
The new approval said: forget the organ.
If the tumor has this molecular feature — MSI-high — give it pembro.
The molecule, not the organ, defines the treatment.
We are going to spend the next segment on this idea, because it is one of the most important conceptual shifts in modern medicine.
For now: PD-L1, TMB, and MSI are the three main biomarkers for checkpoint inhibitor response.
PD-L1 is widely used but imperfect.
TMB is useful but messy.
MSI is the cleanest signal we have.
And the cleanest signal is the one that gave us the first tumor-agnostic drug approval, which is where we'll go after the break.
